attributes.stem: A 28-year-old Aboriginal female is admitted to hospital with a two-month history of joint pain and fatigue. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 150/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. She has oral ulcers, non-scarring alopecia and synovitis in the small joints in both hands. Investigations reveal multiple abnormalities including positive antinuclear antibodies with a titre of 1:640 and sub-nephrotic range proteinuria. Following confirmation of the diagnosis on renal biopsy, she receives intravenous methylprednisolone followed by a high-dose oral prednisolone tail, hydroxychloroquine, and mycophenolate sodium.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following medications is LEAST appropriate to be initially commenced alongside steroids?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) including oral ulcers, alopecia, arthritis, and lupus nephritis confirmed on biopsy. She is being treated with high-dose corticosteroids and immunosuppression, which necessitates several preventive medications.

Dapagliflozin is a sodium-glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitor used for various indications including diabetes management and reduction of proteinuria in various forms of kidney disease. Whilst close monitoring of BSLs (especially in the afternoon) is vital in patients commenced on high-dose steroids, immediately commencing an agent such as an SGLT2 inhibitor is inappropriate given the potential for risks without definitive confirmation of persistent hyperglycaemia. Such risks of SGLT2 inhibitors include urinary tract infections, which may be particularly harmful in an already immunosuppressed patient. Of course, if monitoring of BSLs revealed steroid-induced hyperglycaemia, an agent such as intermediate-acting insulin may be appropriate in the first instance, in consultation with an endocrinologist.

Esomeprazole is appropriate as prophylaxis against steroid-induced reflux and gastric ulceration, which is a significant risk with high-dose corticosteroids. Proton pump inhibitors are recommended for all patients receiving high-dose steroids.

Colecalciferol (vitamin D) and calcium carbonate are essential for minimising the risk of steroid-induced osteoporosis. All patients on long-term corticosteroids should receive calcium and vitamin D supplementation unless contraindicated.

Trimethoprim / Sulfamethoxazole is appropriate as prophylaxis against Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, a severe opportunistic infection in immunosuppressed patients. This is particularly important given the combination of high-dose steroids and mycophenolate.

The management of lupus nephritis requires careful attention to preventing complications of both the disease and its treatment. While immunosuppression is necessary to control disease activity, preventive measures against infection, osteoporosis, and gastro-oesophageal reflux disease are equally important aspects of care.

With regards to the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander population, there exists an increased incidence of SLE compared to non-Indigenous people. There also exists an increased rate of lupus nephritis as a manifestation of SLE, as well as increased rates of progression to end-stage kidney disease in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people with lupus nephritis.
learningPoints: While immunosuppression is necessary to control disease activity in lupus nephritis, preventive measures against infection, osteoporosis, and gastro-oesophageal reflux disease are equally important aspects of care
There are higher rates of SLE and lupus nephritis in the Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander population.
references: Hodgens A, Sharman T. Corticosteroids. In: StatPearls [online]. 2023. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK554612/

* Eades LE et al. Systemic lupus erythematosus in Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples in Australia: addressing disparities and barriers to optimising patient care. The Lancet Rheumatology. Volume 6, Issue 10, e713 - e726. Available at: 10.1016/S2665-9913(24)00095-X
